summary: Business-capability centric teams are long-lived teams aligned to specific business areas like order management or billing, rather than temporary project teams. These teams own their domain end-to-end—thinking, building, and running their systems—without handoffs to other teams. While this approach may require slightly more headcount than project-centric models, it preserves architectural integrity by avoiding the shortcuts and technical debt that project teams create when rushing to deliver scope. The key to success is ensuring teams are outcome-oriented toward business metrics rather than just delivering features. Business capabilities can be categorized as strategic (custom-built) or utility (buy and configure), with different staffing and delivery approaches for each.
key_points: Business-capability centric teams are permanent, aligned to business areas, and own their systems end-to-end without handoffs for testing, deployment, or support
Project-centric models compromise architectural integrity through shortcuts like ad-hoc integrations and quick-and-dirty code because teams only care about delivering scope by deadline
Conway's Law supports this model—single teams owning multiple related components enables high cohesion and low coupling between teams
Teams must be outcome-oriented toward business metrics to avoid devolving into mere scope-delivery machines
Utility capabilities (HR, payroll, legal) can use packaged software with 'buy-it, configure-it, run-it' teams, while strategic capabilities need custom development
money_quotes: Business-capability centric teams are 'think-it, build-it and run-it' teams. They do not hand over to other teams for testing, deploying or supporting what they build.
Unless they are empowered to work towards a business outcome, they may devolve to being scope-delivery oriented.
The project-centric model usually ends up compromising the architectural integrity of the application landscape because each project team only cares about delivering its scope by the promised date.
The lack of work-in-process limits at a project portfolio level leads to many projects getting started and few finishing or delivering desired outcomes.
Conway's law isn't against a single team being responsible for multiple related components. It allows for high cohesion of component ownership and low coupling between teams.
It is a non-trivial exercise to draw boundaries within the application landscape and parcel it out to teams.
key_insight: Long-lived teams aligned to business capabilities preserve architectural integrity and enable true ownership, while project-centric models inevitably create technical debt through misaligned incentives and constant team churn.
